hero_quote: Belarus is a land of rich history, unique nature and places that are worth seeing with your own eyes.
place_mir_desc: One of the most famous architectural landmarks of Belarus.
place_brest_desc: A symbol of courage and memory of the Great Patriotic War.
place_bialowieza_desc: A unique nature reserve of global importance.
advantages_title: Why is it worth visiting Belarus?
advantage1_desc: Castles, fortresses and ancient towns with centuries of history.
advantage2_desc: Reserves, lakes and forests that have kept their pristine look.
advantage3_desc: A warm welcome and national dishes for every taste.
advantage4_desc: A well-developed transport network and affordable tour prices.
discover_title: Immerse yourself in the culture of Belarus
discover_text: Belarus combines a rich history, unique architecture and centuries-old traditions.
discover_li2: 🏛 Hundreds of museums and historical sites
regions_subtitle: Click on a region to learn more about it
route1_desc: Mir and Nesvizh Castles — a journey through history and architecture.
route2_desc: A tour of the Brest Fortress.
route3_desc: Białowieża Forest, lakes and unique nature trails.
route4_desc: A tour of the scenic lakes of northern Belarus and the Repin estate museum.
route5_desc: St. Sophia Cathedral and ancient monuments of the first state on Belarusian lands.
route6_desc: A tour of Gomel region: the Rumyantsev-Paskevich palace and the history of the region.
cat_mir_desc: A unique architectural monument of the 16th century.
cat_nesvizh_desc: One of the most famous castles in Belarus. Included in the UNESCO World Heritage List.
cat_brest_desc: A memorial complex on the site of the heroic defence of 1941.
cat_bialowieza_desc: One of the oldest forests in Europe, a bison nature reserve.
cat_belaya_vezha_desc: An ancient 13th-century defensive tower in the town of Kamyanyets.
cat_sofia_desc: One of the oldest churches on the territory of Belarus.
cat_repin_desc: The former estate of artist Ilya Repin near Vitebsk.
cat_gomel_desc: The residence of the Rumyantsevs and Paskevichs with a park on the bank of the Sozh river.
cat_grodno_desc: The residence of Grand Dukes of Lithuania and Kings of Poland.
cat_novogrudok_desc: Ruins of the castle — the first capital of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania.
cat_khatyn_desc: A monument to all Belarusian villages burned during the war.
cat_uppertown_desc: The historic centre of Minsk with 17th–18th century architecture.
cat_mogilev_desc: A symbol of city self-governance, a restored architectural monument.
cat_bobruisk_desc: One of the largest fortresses of the Russian Empire in the 19th century.
cat_artmuseum_desc: The largest collection of works of art in Belarus.

region_minsk_coat_caption: Coat of arms of Minsk Region
region_minsk_coat2_caption: Coat of arms of Minsk city
region_minsk_text: Minsk Region occupies a central position in Belarus and has historically been considered its core. The first mentions of Minsk date back to the 11th century. The city arose at the crossroads of trade routes and soon became one of the largest centres in the region. Around it formed political, economic and cultural ties that subsequently determined the development of the entire country. As part of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, Minsk received Magdeburg rights and became an important administrative centre. A special role in the region's history was played by the Radziwill magnate family, who owned vast lands. Their main residence was Nesvizh, where one of the most beautiful castles in Eastern Europe was built. After the partitions of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, the region joined the Russian Empire. In the 19th century railways passed through Minsk, accelerating the development of industry and trade. In the 20th century the city became the capital of the Byelorussian SSR and then of independent Republic of Belarus. As a result, Minsk Region gradually became the main political and economic centre of the country.
region_brest_coat_caption: Coat of arms of Brest Region
region_brest_text: The history of Brest Region begins in the early Middle Ages. The southern part of the region was part of the Turov Principality — one of the largest principalities of Ancient Rus. Important cities were Turov, Pinsk and Berestye (modern Brest). In the 11th–12th centuries Turov was considered one of the main political and religious centres of Rus, and the local princely dynasty was connected to the descendants of Prince Vladimir the Saint. In the 13th–14th centuries these lands became part of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania. Thanks to its position on the border of the Slavic and Western European worlds, Brest quickly became an important trade centre. In the 16th century one of the most important events of Eastern European religious history took place here — the Union of Brest of 1596, which gave rise to the Greek Catholic (Uniate) Church. After the partitions of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth in the late 18th century, the region joined the Russian Empire. In the 19th century the Brest Fortress was built — one of the largest fortresses on the western border of the Empire. In 1921, under the Treaty of Riga, the territory became part of Poland. In 1939 it was annexed to the Byelorussian SSR. The Brest Region became known worldwide in the summer of 1941 for the heroic defence of the Brest Fortress, which was one of the first to face the assault of German forces.
region_grodno_coat_caption: Coat of arms of Grodno Region
region_grodno_text: Grodno Region has historically been the westernmost region of Belarus and a meeting place of Slavic, Baltic and Polish cultures. In ancient times Baltic tribes of Yotvingians and Lithuanians lived here, as well as Eastern Slavs. The city of Grodno is first mentioned in the 12th century as an important fortress on the Neman River. After the formation of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, Grodno became one of the most important residences of the Grand Dukes. In the 15th–16th centuries the city experienced a true flourishing. Rulers of the state frequently resided here, and diets and diplomatic negotiations took place. King Stephen Báthory made Grodno his favourite residence and died here in 1586. In the era of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, the region remained one of the most important political centres of the state. It was in Grodno in 1793 that the last Sejm of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth was held, effectively ratifying its partition. After joining the Russian Empire the region retained strong Polish and Catholic influence. In the interwar period most of the region was part of Poland. Today Grodno Region is known for its rich architectural heritage, castles and multicultural history.
region_vitebsk_coat_caption: Coat of arms of Vitebsk Region
region_vitebsk_text: Vitebsk Region is considered the cradle of Belarusian statehood. It was here that the Polotsk Principality arose — the first powerful state formation on the territory of modern Belarus. As early as the 9th century, Polotsk was a major political and trade centre on the route 'from the Varangians to the Greeks'. The principality reached its greatest flourishing under Vseslav the Sorcerer in the 11th century. Polotsk was so influential that it rivalled Kyiv and Novgorod. St. Sophia Cathedral was built here — one of the three oldest Sophia cathedrals of the Eastern Slavs alongside those in Kyiv and Novgorod. Outstanding figures such as Euphrosyne of Polotsk — one of the most venerated saints of Belarus — lived and worked in Vitebsk Region. In the 13th–14th centuries the Polotsk lands became part of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania and retained their importance as a centre of trade with the Baltic. After the first partition of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth in 1772, a significant part of Vitebsk Region was among the first Belarusian lands to join the Russian Empire. During the Great Patriotic War the region became one of the largest centres of the partisan movement.
region_mogilev_coat_caption: Coat of arms of Mogilev Region
region_mogilev_text: The history of Mogilev Region is closely linked to its position on the eastern border of Belarusian lands. In ancient times Krivichs and Radimichs lived here. The most important cities were Mogilev, Mstislavl and Bobruisk. Trade routes connecting the Russian principalities with western lands passed through the region. As part of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, Mogilev quickly grew into a large trading city. Crafts flourished, merchants from various countries worked here, and Orthodox and Catholic churches were built. In the 17th century the city repeatedly became an arena of wars between the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth and the Muscovite state. After the first partition of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth in 1772, a significant part of the region was among the first to join the Russian Empire. In the 19th century Mogilev became a provincial centre and an important administrative hub. During the Great Patriotic War the region found itself in the path of the main German offensive. The defence of Mogilev in the summer of 1941, which delayed the advance of the Wehrmacht eastward, is particularly well known.
region_gomel_coat_caption: Coat of arms of Gomel Region
region_gomel_text: The territory of modern Gomel Region was in ancient times inhabited by the Radimichs — an East Slavic tribe living along the banks of the Sozh River. For a long time the region was on the borderland of various Rus principalities. The largest cities gradually became Gomel, Mozyr and Rechytsa. From the 14th century the region became part of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania. Important river trade routes along the Dnieper, Pripyat and Sozh, linking the Baltic and Black Seas, passed through its territory. After the Union of Lublin the region became part of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth. After the partitions of Poland, Gomel Region joined the Russian Empire. In the 19th century Gomel became one of the largest industrial and railway centres of the region. During the Soviet period the region developed actively as a centre of machine-building and the chemical industry. However, the most significant event of its recent history was the Chernobyl disaster of 1986. The southern districts of the region received heavy radioactive contamination, many villages were evacuated, and the consequences of the accident are still felt today.
modal_mir_address: Krasnoarmeyskaya St. 2, Mir, Korelichi District, Grodno Region
modal_mir_desc: A unique monument of 16th-century defensive architecture and an outstanding example of secular Gothic. Inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List. Today the castle houses a museum and hosts festivals and guided tours.
modal_nesvizh_desc: A palace-castle complex of the Radziwill family, founded in 1583. One of the most famous architectural ensembles in Belarus, a UNESCO World Heritage Site surrounded by a picturesque park.
modal_brest_address: Heroes of the Defence of the Brest Fortress St., Brest
modal_brest_desc: A memorial complex on the site of the fortress whose defence became a symbol of resilience in the first days of the Great Patriotic War. It includes the Defence Museum, the Courage monument and the Kholm Gate.
modal_bialowieza_desc: One of the oldest and largest primeval forests in Europe and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The reserve is famous for its bison population, nature museum and the residence of Ded Moroz (Father Frost).
modal_bela_vezha_desc: A 13th-century defensive keep, one of the few surviving monuments of Old Russian defensive architecture. The tower is about 30 metres tall and houses a museum inside.
modal_sofia_desc: One of the oldest stone churches on the territory of Belarus, founded in the 11th century modelled on the Sophia of Kyiv. Today the cathedral houses a museum and a concert hall for organ music.
modal_repin_address: Zdravnyovo village, Vitebsk District, Vitebsk Region
modal_repin_desc: The former estate of the renowned artist Ilya Repin, where he lived and worked from 1892 to 1900. Features a restored manor house, a park and a museum exhibition dedicated to the life and work of the artist.
modal_gomel_desc: The residence of the Princes Rumyantsev and Paskevich from the 18th–19th centuries, featuring a magnificent palace, the Cathedral of Saints Peter and Paul and an extensive park on the bank of the Sozh River.
modal_grodno_desc: The ancient residence of the Grand Dukes of Lithuania and Kings of Poland, founded as far back as the 11th century. The castle grounds today house a historical and archaeological museum.
modal_novogrudok_desc: Ruins of the first capital of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania. Castle Hill with the remains of fortress towers is one of the most atmospheric historic sites in Belarus.
modal_khatyn_desc: A memorial on the site of the village burned in 1943, a symbol of remembrance for all Belarusian villages destroyed along with their inhabitants during the Great Patriotic War.
modal_uppertown_address: Nyamiha St. / Kiryla i Mefodiya St., Minsk
modal_uppertown_desc: The historic centre of Minsk with 17th–18th century buildings: the Town Hall, the Holy Spirit Cathedral, the Cathedral and charming cobbled streets of the old city.
modal_mogilev_desc: A restored 17th-century architectural monument and symbol of Mogilev's civic self-governance. The tower's observation deck offers a panoramic view of the historic city centre.
modal_bobruisk_desc: One of the largest fortresses of the Russian Empire in the 19th century, which played an important role in the Patriotic War of 1812. Earthworks, casemates and powder magazines have been preserved.
modal_artmuseum_desc: The largest collection of fine art in Belarus: icon painting, paintings from the 17th–20th centuries, sculpture and decorative and applied arts.
